note_data.title: Statements of cash flows
keyPoints: Financial statements provide information about the financial position, financial performance & cash flows of an entity
Financial statements must include information about assets, liabilities, equity, income & expenses, contributions by & distributions to owners, and cash flows
Manufacturing accounts are prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
Manufacturing accounts only include information about the factory & actual manufacturing process
All non-production costs (administration, finance, & distribution costs) are recorded in the Income Statement
Cost of production = prime cost + factory overheads
Transfer price = production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
Factory/manufacturing profit = difference between transfer price & production cost of completed goods

definitions.definition: An account prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
The total of direct materials, direct labour, & direct expenses
Costs incurred from the running of the factory, including indirect factory wages & depreciation of factory machinery
Inventory of partly finished goods in the factory at any point in time
Percentage added to the factory cost of production to arrive at the transfer price
The production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
Profit which is not recognised until

howToAnswer.instruction: Create a full cash flow statement with three sections: operating, investing, and financing activities
Show workings for each cash flow figure, adjusting for non-cash items and working capital changes
Give the classification reason, e.g. 'This is an operating activity because it relates to the main revenue-producing activities'

modelAnswer.question: Classify the following items in a cash flow statement: (a) Purchase of machinery $50,000 (b) Dividends paid $15,000 (c) Receipts from customers $200,000
modelAnswer.answer: (a) Purchase of machinery $50,000 - Investing activity (cash outflow). This is the acquisition of a non-current asset. (b) Dividends paid $15,000 - Financing activity (cash outflow). This represents a distribution to equity holders. (c) Receipts from customers $200,000 - Operating activity (cash inflow). This relates to the main revenue-producing activities of the business.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Purchase of machinery classified as investing activity
Mark 2: Correct reason - acquisition of non-current asset
Mark 3: Dividends classified as financing activity
Mark 4: Correct reason - distribution to equity holders
Mark 5: Customer receipts classified as operating activity
Mark 6: Correct reason - main revenue-producing activities

commonMistakes: Including non-production costs in the manufacturing account instead of the Income Statement
Confusing prime cost with total production cost
Mixing up factory overheads with general business expenses
examinerTips: Remember that manufacturing accounts only deal with factory costs and production processes
Always separate direct costs (prime cost) from indirect costs (factory overheads) when calculating cost of production
formulas: Operating cash flow (indirect) = Net profit + Depreciation +/- Working capital changes
Cash from customers = Sales + Opening receivables - Closing receivables
Cash to suppliers = Purchases + Closing payables - Opening payables
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Three-section cash flow statement showing Operating Activities (using direct or indirect method), Investing Activities (asset purchases/disposals), and Financing Activities (equity/debt transactions), with net increase/decrease in cash leading to closing cash balance
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare a cash flow statement from given balance sheets and additional information about non-current asset purchases
Featured in O/N 2022 P2 Q5 (6 marks) — required calculation of cash flows from operating activities using the indirect method with adjustments for depreciation and working capital changes
Appeared in M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (10 marks) — comprehensive cash flow statement preparation including classification of financing activities like loan receipts and dividend payments
Tested in O/N 2021 P2 Q4 (7 marks) — focused on identifying and categorizing cash flows into operating, investing, and financing activities from a list of transactions

workedExample.question: Prepare a statement of cash flows for ABC Ltd for the year ended 31 December 2023. Net profit: $45,000, Depreciation: $8,000, Increase in inventory: $3,000, Decrease in trade receivables: $2,000, Increase in trade payables: $1,500, Purchase of equipment: $12,000, Bank loan received: $10,000. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Start with net profit $45,000 as the base for operating activities
Step 3: Adjust for working capital changes: subtract inventory increase $3,000, add receivables decrease $2,000, add payables increase $1,500
Step 4: Calculate net cash from operating activities: $45,000 + $8,000 - $3,000 + $2,000 + $1,500 = $53,500
Step 5: Record investing activities: equipment purchase $(12,000)
Step 6: Record financing activities: bank loan $10,000
Step 7: Calculate net increase in cash: $53,500 - $12,000 + $10,000 = $51,500
workedExample.answer: Cash flows from operating activities: Net profit $45,000, Add: Depreciation $8,000, Less: Increase in inventory $(3,000), Add: Decrease in receivables $2,000, Add: Increase in payables $1,500. Net cash from operating activities $53,500. Cash flows from investing activities: Purchase of equipment $(12,000). Cash flows from financing activities: Bank loan received $10,000. Net increase in cash $51,500.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct format, 2 marks for operating activities calculation, 1 mark for each working capital adjustment, 1 mark for investing activities, 1 mark for financing activities, 1 mark for net cash calculation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused cash receipts with profit figures' — always distinguish between profit (accrual basis) and actual cash movements when preparing cash flow statements

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently misclassified loan repayments as operating expenses rather than financing activities' — ensure transactions are categorized correctly into operating, investing, or financing activities

Examiner Report 2023: 'Common error was failing to add back depreciation when using indirect method' — remember depreciation is a non-cash expense that must be added back to net profit in operating activities
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw a three-section cash flow statement format: (1) Operating Activities section showing net profit, adjustments for non-cash items (+ depreciation, +/- working capital changes), (2) Investing Activities section showing cash flows from buying/selling non-current assets, (3) Financing Activities section showing cash flows from loans, share issues, and dividend payments. Include arrows showing how each section flows to 'Net increase/decrease in cash' at the bottom. Label clearly whether items are additions (+) or subtractions (-) in each section.
note_data.quickRecap: Manufacturing accounts calculate production costs by adding factory overheads to prime cost. They only include factory-related costs, while other business expenses go to the Income Statement. Transfer price includes a mark-up on production cost, creating manufacturing profit.
